description: This figure presents a comprehensive quantitative decomposition of strengthening mechanisms in Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels, specifically illustrating the calculated contributions to yield strength for Fe-6.8Al alloy across different thermomechanical processing conditions. The chart compares three material states: 98% cold-rolled (CR), and isothermally annealed at 750°C for 5 min and 60 min. It displays the individual contributions from solid solution strengthening (~95.6 MPa, constant), lattice frictional stress (41 MPa), order strengthening from nano-sized Fe3Al D03 ordered phases (2.1–3.0 nm diameter, volume fraction varying with Al content), Hall-Petch grain boundary strengthening (varying with grain size from 25.2 ± 5.0 μm to 48.3 ± 1.2 μm), and Taylor dislocation strengthening (dominant in CR state with density ~2.2 × 10^15 m^-2, negligible after recrystallization). The experimental validation shows predicted yield strengths matching measured values of 1800 MPa (CR), 700 MPa (5 min), and 265 MPa (60 min), demonstrating the transition from dislocation-based to grain-boundary and ordered-phase controlled strengthening mechanisms during recrystallization and grain growth.

Figure 1 presents Mössbauer spectroscopy data for Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels, comprising transmission spectra (left column: a, c, e, g, i) plotted as transmission percentage versus velocity (mm/s), and corresponding hyperfine magnetic field distributions P(Bhf) (right column: b, d, f, h, j) plotted as probability versus Bhf (T). The characterized materials include α-Fe foil as a reference standard and powder samples of Fe-5Al, Fe-6Al, Fe-6.8Al, and Fe-9Al alloys annealed at 600°C. The left panels display characteristic six-line magnetic hyperfine splitting patterns where increasing Al content induces progressive spectral broadening and reduced dip depth. The right panels show the deconvoluted P(Bhf) distributions (green curves representing individual Gaussian components) revealing distinct local iron environments: a dominant disordered α-Fe matrix peak (~33 T), an ordered Fe3Al D03 phase contribution (~30.6 T), and a minor B2-type FeAl peak (~23 T) emerging at higher Al concentrations. The distributions demonstrate that with increasing aluminum content from 5 to 9 wt%, the hyperfine field distribution broadens and shifts toward lower fields, indicating enhanced atomic ordering and progressive formation of nano-sized ordered precipitates.

Figure 2 presents two-dimensional small-angle X-ray scattering (2D-SAXS) profiles for Fe-Al based low-density steels with varying aluminum content: (a) Fe-5Al, (b) Fe-6Al, (c) Fe-6.8Al, and (d) Fe-9Al. Each subfigure displays a concentric circular intensity pattern centered on the direct beam position, characteristic of scattering from nano-sized heterogeneities in the material. The color gradient from cyan (low intensity) through green and yellow to red (high intensity) represents the scattered X-ray intensity distribution. These patterns reveal the presence of nano-sized ordered Fe3Al (D03) precipitates dispersed in the ferrite matrix. With increasing aluminum content from 5 wt% to 9 wt%, the scattering intensity increases and the ring sharpness becomes more pronounced, indicating a progressive increase in both the volume fraction and the average size of the ordered phase particles, quantified elsewhere as growing from approximately 2.5 nm to 3.75 nm. This data supports the identification of ordered phase strengthening as a key mechanism in these alloys.

Figure 3 presents small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) profiles showing the logarithmic scattering intensity as a function of the scattering vector q (nm⁻¹) for Fe-Al based low-density steels with varying aluminum contents: Fe-5AL (circles), Fe-6AL (squares), Fe-6.8AL (triangles), and Fe-9AL (diamonds). The open symbols represent experimental data, while the solid blue lines indicate fitted curves using a local monodisperse model with log-normal distribution (Eq. 3). The curves are vertically offset for clarity. The broken dotted lines proportional to q⁻⁴ indicate Porod scattering behavior from sharp interfaces. With increasing Al content from 5 to 9 wt%, the SAXS intensity systematically increases and the Guinier region shifts to lower q values, indicating larger nano-sized ordered Fe₃Al (D₀₃) precipitates. The fitted results reveal particle diameters increasing from 2.5±0.15 nm (Fe-5AL) to 3.75±0.2 nm (Fe-9AL), with corresponding increases in volume fraction. This quantitative nanostructural evidence supports the strengthening mechanism analysis by confirming the evolution of ordered phase characteristics with composition.

This dual-axis plot illustrates the evolution of nano-sized Fe3Al ordered phase characteristics as a function of aluminum content (5–9 wt%) in Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels. The left y-axis tracks volume fraction (0–3%) represented by solid circles, while the right y-axis indicates particle diameter (0–4 nm) shown as solid squares with error bars. Both parameters demonstrate a clear increasing trend with aluminum addition: the ordered phase diameter grows from approximately 2.5 nm at 5 wt% Al to 3.75 nm at 9 wt% Al, while the volume fraction rises from ~0.4% to ~2.0%. These quantitative results, obtained from Small Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS) analysis and validated by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) observations cited in the text, reveal that higher aluminum concentrations promote the formation of larger and more abundant D03-structured Fe3Al precipitates. This correlation establishes the foundation for the order strengthening mechanism, where these coherent nano-sized ordered phases restrict dislocation mobility through antiphase boundary formation, contributing significantly to the yield strength especially at higher aluminum levels.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrograph displaying the fully recrystallized microstructure of Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel after isothermal annealing at 750 °C for 60 min. The image reveals equiaxed ferrite grains with well-defined grain boundaries, indicating complete recrystallization following 98% cold rolling deformation. The average grain size is reported as 48.3 ± 1.2 μm, demonstrating significant grain growth compared to the 5 min annealed condition (25.2 ± 5.0 μm) referenced in the study. The microstructure exhibits a homogeneous distribution of polygonal grains with visible contrast variations between grains due to crystallographic orientation differences. The scale bar indicates 300 μm. This micrograph provides direct evidence of grain coarsening during prolonged annealing and supports the quantitative grain size measurements used in Hall-Petch strengthening calculations. The image confirms the transition from deformed microstructure to equiaxed recrystallized grains, essential for understanding the evolution of mechanical properties.

This figure presents a dark-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph of the Fe-6.8Al (Fe-6.8 wt% Al) ferritic low-density steel, specifically from the sample cold-rolled to 98% reduction and subsequently annealed at 750°C for 5 minutes. The main micrograph displays the spatial distribution of nanoscale ordered precipitates (highlighted by red arrows) appearing as bright contrast regions uniformly dispersed within the ferrite matrix, with a scale bar indicating 200 nm. The upper-right inset shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern recorded along the [011] zone axis of the Fe-Al matrix, where strong fundamental reflections are indexed as {200} and {011}, while the weak superlattice spots (indicated by yellow arrows) confirm the presence of ordered D03-Fe3Al phase. The dark-field image was acquired using the 00-type weak superlattice reflections, revealing extremely fine ordered particles with an average size of approximately 2.1 ± 0.2 nm. This direct visualization provides critical evidence for the order-strengthening mechanism in Fe-Al based steels, supporting the quantitative SAXS and Mössbauer spectroscopy data regarding the size, volume fraction, and coherent nature of the nano-ordered precipitates that contribute significantly to the alloy's yield strength.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) line profile analysis results for Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel under three processing conditions: 98% cold-rolled (98%CR, black curves), and isothermally annealed at 750°C for 5 min (red curves) and 60 min (blue curves). Panel (a) displays the normalized intensity of the (110) diffraction peak, showing substantial peak broadening in the 98%CR sample due to lattice micro-strain from severe cold deformation, with progressive peak sharpening upon annealing indicating recrystallization; the inset shows complete XRD profiles confirming the single-phase ferrite (BCC) structure with indexed reflections including (110), (200), (211), (220), and (310). Panels (b), (c), and (d) present the variance method analysis, plotting the second-order moment (M₂, solid lines) and normalized fourth-order moment (M₄/q², dashed lines) as functions of the scattering vector q for the 98%CR, 750°C/5min, and 750°C/60min conditions, respectively, used to separate size and strain contributions. Panel (e) shows the calculated dislocation density evolution, revealing a dramatic three-order-of-magnitude decrease from approximately 2×10¹⁵ m⁻² in the cold-rolled state to ~10¹² m⁻² after 60 min annealing, confirming that recrystallization drastically reduces dislocation density and lattice micro-strain (from 1.46×10⁻³ to 1.46×10⁻⁴).

The figure presents mechanical property curves for Fe-6.8Al low-density steel subjected to 98% cold rolling (CR) followed by isothermal annealing at 750 °C for 5 and 60 min. Panel (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves comparing three conditions: the 98% CR state (black curve) exhibits an ultra-high yield strength of approximately 1800 MPa but fails prematurely with minimal uniform elongation (~1.2%); the 5 min annealed sample (red curve) shows balanced properties with yield strength (YS) of 700 MPa, ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 870 MPa, and 20% elongation; while the 60 min annealed condition (blue curve) demonstrates lower strength (YS 265 MPa, UTS 394 MPa) but superior ductility (29.8% elongation). Panel (b) illustrates the rate of strain hardening versus true strain for the annealed samples, revealing that the 60 min sample maintains higher strain hardening rates over a broader strain range compared to the 5 min sample, correlating with its enhanced uniform elongation. These results demonstrate that short-duration annealing (5 min) achieves an optimal combination of high strength and reasonable ductility through partial recrystallization, whereas extended annealing (60 min) leads to significant grain growth and softening.

This bar graph quantifies the individual contributions of five distinct strengthening mechanisms to the yield strength (YS) of Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel under three processing conditions: 98% cold rolling (CR), and isothermal annealing at 750°C for 5 min and 60 min. The three stacked bars represent total yield strengths of 1800 MPa, 700 MPa, and 265 MPa, respectively. The legend identifies the mechanisms as dislocations (magenta), grain boundary (blue), ordering (green), solid solution (black), and lattice frictional stress (red). In the 98% CR state, dislocation strengthening and grain boundary strengthening provide the dominant contributions, consistent with the high dislocation density (~10^15 m^-2) and fine crystallite size (~102 nm) reported in the text. Upon annealing, the contributions from dislocations and grain boundaries decrease significantly due to recovery/recrystallization and grain growth (to 25.2 µm at 5 min and 48.3 µm at 60 min), while solid solution and ordering contributions remain relatively stable. This figure mechanistically explains the dramatic strength reduction from severe work hardening to softened recrystallized states.
